title: practical english usage - Part 345
content:
‘ I've got a bit of a headache.' T beg your pardon? (     .. .-Please?) We   do not use please to ask people what they have said.

2 when     please       is  not   used 560 vocabulary' •         prefer Prefer can also be followed by an infinitive (this is normal after would prefer). -» She prefers making toys for her children rather than buying them.

I prefer riding to walking, (     I-prefer riding to-w&lk-) The  second can be introduced by to or rather than (more formal).

When  we  say that we  prefer one activity to another, two -ing forms can be used.

practically,          nearly          almost 17 and                      ►   ʒ possibility             opportunity It's the company's policy to employ a certain number of disabled people.

It's not my policy to believe everything I hear.

After the war, British foreign policy was rather confused.

with politics).

Policy is used for people’ s rules of behaviour (not necessarily connected You talk beautifully - you should be in politics.

I don't know much  about politics, but I don't think this is a democracy.

government and related ideas.

Politics (usually singular but always with -5, ►     117.3) is used to talk about 559  politics        and   policy political history a political career Political means ‘ connected with politics'.

I don't think it would be politic to ask for a loan just now.

Politic is a rather formal word for 'wise', ‘ prudent’ .

558  politic       and   political From the employers' point of view, this would cause a lot of problems.

- In Professor Lucas's opinion, everybody should work a 20-hour week.

You have to judge a school from the child's point of view.

- In my view, it's a pretty good school.

He wrote about the war from the point of view of the ordinary soldier.

- In my opinion, war is always wrong. (= I think war ...) somebody is affected by what happens. Compare: example as a student, a woman, a Greek or a Catholic), and is used to say how view/opinion. It usually means ‘ as seen from somebody's position in life' (for But from somebody's point of view is not quite the same as in somebody's Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 561 vocabulary •         presently ►    591 and quickly,       soon         early She's a girl with very strong principles.

(     .. . the principalof universal gravitation/) Newton discovered the principle of universal gravitation.

A  principle is a scientific law or a moral rule.

If you want to leave early, you 'll have to ask the Principal.

for adults).

The  noun principal means ‘ head of a school’  (especially, in Britain, of a school (xni . . . your principle reason ...) What's your principal reason for wanting to be a doctor?

‘ main’ , 'most important’ .

These two words have the same pronunciation. The  adjective principal means 563 principal         and   principle^ not She received the Nobel prize for physics, (     .. . the Nobel price-...) not What's the price of the green dress? (     . . . the prize of the green dress?) you have done something exceptional, or if you win a competition.

The  price is what you pay if you buy something. A  prize is what you are given if 562 price      and   prize       _______ ►    460 and pretty,      fairly,     quite         rather ‘ Can I have an ice cream? ‘ Presently, dear.' He's having a rest now. He'll be down presently.

short answer.

With this meaning, presently usually comes in end-position, or separately as a Another meaning of presently is ‘ not now, later', ‘ in a minute’ .

The Manager is presently in Canada, but he will contact you on his return.

Professor Holloway is presently working on plant diseases.

with the verb (►  200).

When  presently means 'now, at present’ , it usually comes in mid-position 561  presently For more about to with -ing forms, ►     10i,2.

I would prefer to stay at home rather than drive/driuing to your mother's.

She prefers to make toys for her children rather than buy/buying them.

in this case.

We   can use an infinitive (without to) or an -ing form after rather than 564 vocabulary •         quite We thought nobody would be there, but actually quite a few people came. -> We're having quite a bit of trouble with the kids just now.
